---
title: heormodel
author: Pedro Nascimento de Lima
---

Health economic evaluation in Python: parameter sampling, simulation across model types, cost-effectiveness analysis, and value-of-information analysis for model-based health technology assessment.

## Install

```bash
pip install heormodel
```

The `calibration` extra adds Bayesian calibration: `pip install "heormodel[calibration]"`.

## Quickstart

A three-state Markov cohort state-transition model comparing treatment with standard care, evaluated by probabilistic sensitivity analysis:

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from heormodel.models import CohortSpec, MarkovModel
from heormodel.params import Beta, Gamma, ParameterSet
from heormodel.run import SeedManager, run_psa
from heormodel.cea import icer_table
from heormodel.report import plot_frontier
from heormodel.voi import evpi


def model(p, strategy):
    p_progress = p["p_progress"] * (p["rr_treat"] if strategy == "Treatment" else 1.0)
    # Transition matrix. Rows: current state. Columns: next state.
    P = np.array([
        [1 - p_progress - p["p_die"], p_progress, p["p_die"]],
        [0.0, 1 - p["p_die_sick"], p["p_die_sick"]],
        [0.0, 0.0, 1.0],
    ])
    cost = np.array([0.0, p["c_sick"], 0.0])
    if strategy == "Treatment":
        cost[:2] += p["c_treat"]
    return CohortSpec(P, cost, np.array([1.0, p["u_sick"], 0.0]))


engine = MarkovModel(states=("Healthy", "Sick", "Dead"),
                     strategies=("Standard care", "Treatment"),
                     model_fn=model, n_cycles=40)

params = ParameterSet({
    "p_progress": Beta(20, 180), "rr_treat": Beta(60, 40),
    "p_die": Beta(5, 995), "p_die_sick": Beta(50, 450),
    "c_sick": Gamma(100, 250.0), "c_treat": Gamma(100, 80.0),
    "u_sick": Beta(150, 50),
})

draws = params.sample(1_000, seed=SeedManager(1).generator())
outcomes = run_psa(engine, draws)
icer_table(outcomes).round(1)
```

The `icer_table` orders strategies by cost and reports incremental cost-effectiveness ratios along the efficiency frontier; the `status` column flags dominated (`D`) and extended-dominated (`ED`) strategies. `plot_frontier` draws the same frontier from mean cost and effect per strategy:

```{python}
plot_frontier(outcomes);
```

The expected value of perfect information reads off the same object:

```{python}
print(f"EVPI at WTP 50,000: {evpi(outcomes, wtp=50_000):,.1f}")
```

## How it fits together

One parameter object flows through swappable model engines into a shared analysis layer. Every engine returns costs and effects in one standardized structure, `Outcomes`, indexed by `(strategy, iteration)`, so `icer_table`, `ceac`, and `evpi` behave the same whether the numbers come from a Markov cohort model, a microsimulation, or a spreadsheet export. You do not need an engine to start: [bring your own table](tutorials/byo-outputs.qmd) instead.

[Engines](concepts/engines.qmd) covers the contract every engine satisfies and the shared iteration index that the value-of-information estimators rely on.

## Where to go next

- [Get started](get-started.qmd): install and run your first analysis.
- [Markov cohort model](tutorials/mdm-cohort.qmd): the standard state-transition model, then the [microsimulation engine](tutorials/microsim.qmd) as the step up in detail.
- [Bring your own outputs](tutorials/byo-outputs.qmd): an external results table through cost-effectiveness analysis, value of information, plots, and a run report.
- [Full pipeline](tutorials/full-pipeline.qmd): parameters, `run_psa`, cost-effectiveness analysis, and value of information end to end.
- [Calibration workflow](tutorials/calibration-workflow.qmd): calibrate some parameters, take the rest from the literature, mix both with `mix_draws`, then run the same analyses.
- [API reference](reference/index.qmd): every public function, generated from docstrings.
- [Changelog](changelog.md): what has shipped.
